items.correct: Cost advantages a firm gains as it grows — average cost per unit falls because fixed costs spread over more units, bulk discounts, and specialisation become possible.
items.misconception: Economies of scale means total costs decrease as the business grows.
items.random_wrong: Economies of scale means a business saves the economy money.
items.why: Total costs RISE with growth — it's AVERAGE cost per unit that falls. Students confuse total and average, missing the precise meaning of the concept.

items.correct: Output per unit of input (per worker, per hour) — measuring efficiency, not total output. A small efficient firm can be more productive than a large inefficient one.
items.misconception: Productivity is the total amount produced by a business.
items.random_wrong: Productivity is the number of employees a business has.
items.why: Total output and productivity are different. A factory of 100 workers producing 1000 units is less productive than 10 workers producing 200 units. The ratio matters, not the total.

items.q: What three Ps are added to make the extended marketing mix (7Ps)?
items.correct: People (staff/service quality), Process (how the service is delivered), and Physical Evidence (tangible cues — uniforms, packaging, branding) — added to make the 4Ps relevant to services marketing.
items.misconception: The 7Ps are just the 4Ps repeated with different words.
items.random_wrong: The 7Ps include Profit, Planning and Performance.
items.why: The extended mix specifically addresses services, where customer experience depends on staff, process and atmospherics — not just product. Students treat 4Ps and 7Ps as interchangeable, missing what services marketing requires.

items.q: Porter's three generic strategies are cost leadership, differentiation and:
items.why: It's Focus. “Diversification” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The 'Blue Ocean' concept was proposed by:
items.why: It's Kim and Mauborgne. “Porter” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many generic strategies did Porter identify?
items.why: The real figure is 3 strategies. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
